sub_topics.title: Retail Trade

instructional_blocks.title: Describing Retail trade
teacher_steps: Put learners in appropriate groups to enable everyone participate
Ask learners to discuss the various items they buy from local community shops (tuckshop, tuntemba, butchery, supermarket) and in what quantities
Let each group present its discussions
Consolidate by summarizing and relate the discussion to the content tips
learner_steps: Discuss items bought from local shops and quantities

instructional_blocks.title: Demonstrating functions of a Retailer
teacher_steps: Split the class into groups to role play the functions of a retailer
Ask each learner to participate fully in their groups by bringing out or stating the functions of a retailer as they observe in the role play
Ask Learners to state what key points they observed after each role play
Consolidate by summarising and relate the role plays to the content tips

instructional_blocks.title: Research on the factors to consider before putting up a retail business
teacher_steps: From the previous assignment given to the learners, in ten (10) minutes allow them to discuss their individual findings in their various groups
Ask each group to present their findings
Consolidate by summarising and relating each group's presentation to the content tips
learner_steps: Conduct research on factors to consider before starting retail business

instructional_blocks.title: Investigating opportunities and challenges of running a retail business
teacher_steps: Introduce the invited speaker to the class and indicate what the speaker will talk about
Let the invited speaker present or give a talk to the class on the opportunities and challenges of running a retail business
Invite questions from the learners to the speaker
After the departure of the guest speaker, give time to the learners to bring out the opportunities and challenges of running a retail business from the presentation
Use the answers from the learners to develop the opportunities and challenges of running a retail business as per content tips
Put up the chart with opportunities and challenges of running a retail business that you prepared in advance, or write them on the board
learner_steps: Identify opportunities and challenges from presentation

instructional_blocks.title: Practising running a retail trade
teacher_steps: Engage administration so that they are aware of this learning activity
Organise capital/money or any other resources to buy assorted goods for resale
Buy different types of goods such as sweets, giggies, biscuits, u-fresh, soft drinks from the local wholesale or get them from the school tuckshop
Give the goods to different learners to sell to their friends in class or outside the class at break time
Let the learners price the goods or negotiate the pricing with the buyers at a profit
Consolidate on the activities of selling and emphasize to the learners that this activity is ongoing
learner_steps: Participate in buying and selling activities

sub_topics.short_notes:
The word 'retail' simply means to 'cut again'. Trade is the buying and selling of goods and service with a view of making profit. Retail trade is therefore, the buying and selling of goods in small quantities to suit the consumer's needs. A person who sells the goods in small quantities is known as a retailer. A retailer is a middleman between the wholesaler or producer and the consumer in the chain of distribution of goods and services.

Functions of a Retailer:
- Buying goods from the wholesaler or manufacturer
- Selling to customers according to their needs
- Breaking bulk; the retailer buys the goods in large quantities from the wholesaler or the manufacturer and then breaks them into smaller quantities to suit the consumer's needs
- Offering variety of goods to meet the consumer's needs
- Displaying goods in retail shops
- Pricing goods before selling them to consumers
- Preparing the goods for sale such as packaging them
- Giving after sale service and pre-sale service
- Offering credit to trusted customers
- Giving advice or personal service to consumers especially on new goods
- Offers delivery service on some goods within a targeted marketing area
- Advertises, promotes, gives special offers for his/her business to the public
- Providing goods in suitable quantity
- Supplying local needs to the consumers
- Acting as a link or middleman in the chain of distribution between the consumers and the wholesaler
- To pass on information to wholesalers on consumers' complaints, suggestions or praises on the products
- Opens his/her shop early and closes late to suit the consumers
- Warehousing/storage of goods awaiting sales

Factors to consider before putting up a retail Business:
- Do a market research – there is need to make sure that there is demand for the products to be selling
- Weigh your possibilities – having a dream to open up a retail business is very good, however, before starting one needs to consider all his/her options
- Make a business plan – when one is sure there is demand for the products, the next step is to make a plan for opening the retail business outlining goals, strategies, and projections to guide your decisions and attract investors if needed
- Competition - analyse existing businesses in your area or niche to identify gaps and opportunities
- Financing (Capital) – when you have a final plan you need to raise funds for your project
- Best location – time to find the perfect location! The location must be strategic
- Adhering to local laws – make sure that you respect all the legal requirements that come with the opening of a retail business
- Adopt technologies – determine what your technological infrastructure will be
- Layout and facilities of the retail business– when you take possession of your brand new space, you need to furnish it and set up great displays
- Source of suppliers – make sure you establish a reliable supply chain
- Gather employees – you must hire competent employees
- Security concerns – you must implement security measures to protect both customers and your goods
- Create marketing strategies – develop a marketing strategy to succeed
- Customer experience - focus on creating a welcoming environment and providing excellent service
- Trading hours – consider what works best for your business
- Banking – ensure that banking facilities are easily accessible

Opportunities of Running a Retail Trade:
- Meeting Consumer Needs: Retail allows you to directly address customer demands
- Flexibility in Niches: You can choose to specialize in specific markets
- Building Customer Loyalty: Retail businesses give you the chance to create meaningful customer relationships
- Innovation and Trendsetting: You can introduce new products, ideas, or shopping experiences
- Financial Growth: A well-run retail business can generate consistent revenue
- Job Creation: Retail businesses often contribute to the community by providing employment opportunities
- E-commerce Integration: You can embrace technology by combining a physical store with an online presence
- It requires minimal costs/ less capital to establish
- It is constantly evolving with new technologies and innovations
- Offers opportunities to tap into global market
- Offers opportunities for diversification
- Offers opportunities for supply chain optimisation
- Makes speedy and independent decision

Challenges of Running a Retail Trade:
- Managing inventory: Striking the right balance between having enough stock to meet customer demand and avoiding overstock
- Competition: The retail sector is highly competitive
- Fluctuating demand: Retail businesses can face unpredictable changes in customer demand
- Rising costs: Costs related to rent, utilities, logistics, and wages can quickly add up
- Customer expectations: With the rise of e-commerce and fast delivery options, customers expect seamless shopping experiences
- Economic factors: Economic downturns, inflation, or changes in government regulations can impact consumer spending
- Technology integration: Adopting new technology can be daunting and costly
- Staffing issues: Recruiting, training, and retaining reliable staff can be a challenge
- Security concerns: Retail businesses are vulnerable to theft, fraud, or cyberattacks

vocabulary.definition: Link between two parties
Buying and selling of goods and services within a country's geographical boundaries
Business activity associated with the sale of goods to the final consumer
A person who buys goods or services for their own use
A person or company that makes goods for sale
A person or company that sells goods in large quantities
A person or business that sells goods to the public in relatively small quantities for use or consumption
The activity of producing, buying, selling, or exchange of goods and services to generate revenue or create value
The amount of money by which revenue from sales exceeds costs in a business
Denoting or relating to commerce that integrates the different methods of interaction available to customers (e.g. online, via a mobile device, in a physical shop, etc.)
The practice of buying large quantities of goods from a manufacturer and then dividing them into smaller lots to sell to retailers or consumers

questions.question: Taonga supermarket is a new retail business located in Kabwe, Zambia. The owner, Mr Kakoma realised that there was no modern supermarket in that area. He further conducted market research and identified a need for a modern supermarket that offers a wide range of products at competitive prices. Mr kakoma secured funding from a local bank. He developed a comprehensive business plan, which included strategies for inventory management, staffing and marketing. The supermarket was designed to provide an excellent shopping experience.
sub_questions: What factors did Mr Kakoma consider first before starting his retail business in the above passage?
What other factors could Mr Kakoma considered apart from the ones identified in question 1 above?
What might be some potential challenges that he could face in operating his supermarket?
How does technology allow the supermarket to operate efficiently and effective?

questions.question: What role do retailers play in the distribution chain?
Design a layout for a retail shop window display to highlight a new product line. Explain how your design would attract customers and increase sales.
Conduct a study to gather customer feedback on a retail shop's services. Based on the results provided, suggest ways of improving customer satisfaction.

Beauty Buzz Fashion is a retail clothing store located in a busy shopping area. The store offers a wide range of clothing and accessories for men, women, and children, catering to diverse tastes and preferences. Beauty Buzz Fashion's target market is fashion-conscious consumers who seek stylish and affordable clothing. The buying team sources products from various suppliers and manufacturers, ensuring an assorted range of styles, sizes, and prices. The team attends fashion shows, reads industry publications, and follows social media influencers to stay informed about the latest trends. They negotiate prices, quantities, and delivery terms with suppliers to ensure competitive pricing and timely delivery. In addition, the stores are designed to provide an attractive and welcoming shopping environment. Sales staff are trained to assist customers, respond to queries, and process transactions efficiently. The store's layout is designed to showcase products in an appealing way, and signs are used to highlight promotions and sales. Furthermore, it uses various marketing channels to promote its products and attract customers. The store has a strong social media presence, with regular posts on social media such as Instagram, Facebook, and Twitter. The store also sends out email newsletters to subscribers, showcasing new arrivals, promotions, and sales. In-store promotions, such as discounts and giveaways, are used to drive sales and engage customers. Finally, it prioritises customer satisfaction, offering services as such repairing products for customers, pre-sale services, returns, exchanges, and refunds. The store's sales staff are trained to provide personalised styling advice and assistance, helping customers to find products that meet their needs. The store also offers a loyalty program, rewarding repeat customers with exclusive discounts and offers.
sub_questions: Describe eight (8) services Beauty Buzz offers to customers

sub_topics.expected_standard: Running a retail business practised successfully

sub_topics.title: Wholesale Trade

instructional_blocks.title: Describing wholesale trade
teacher_steps: Put learners in appropriate groups to enable everyone participate
learners to discuss where the various items retailers (tuntemba, butchery, supermarket) sell come from and in what quantities
Let each group present its discussions
Consolidate by summarizing and relate the discussion to the content tips
learner_steps: Identify sources of goods for retailers

instructional_blocks.title: Demonstrating functions of a wholesaler
teacher_steps: Split the class into groups to role play the functions of a wholesaler
Ask each learner to participate fully in their groups by bringing out or stating the functions of a wholesaler as they observe in the role play
Ask Learners to state what key points they observed after each role play
Consolidate by summarizing and relate the role plays to the content tips
learner_steps: Participate in role play as wholesalers

instructional_blocks.title: Investigating opportunities and challenges of running a wholesale business
teacher_steps: Introduce the invited speaker to the class and indicate what the speaker will talk about
Let the invited speaker present or give a talk to the class on the opportunities and challenges of running wholesale business
Invite questions from the learners to the speaker
After the departure of the guest speaker, give time to the learners to bring out the opportunities and challenges
Use the answers from the learners to develop the opportunities and challenges of running a wholesale business as per content tips
Put up the chart with opportunities and challenges that you prepared in advance, or write them on the board

instructional_blocks.title: Practising running a wholesale business
teacher_steps: Engage administration so that they are aware of this learning activity
Organise capital/money or any other resources to buy assorted goods for resale
Buy different types of goods such as cases of sweets, cases of giggies, boxes of biscuits, case of u-fresh, case of soft drinks from the supplier or local producers
Give the cases of goods to different learners to sell to the tuck shop or outside at break time in large quantities
Let the learners price the goods or negotiate the pricing with the buyers at a profit
Consolidate on the activities of selling
learner_steps: Participate in wholesale buying and selling

sub_topics.short_notes:
The word 'whole' simply means bulk and to sale is simply to transact. Trade is the buying and selling of goods and service with a view of making profit. Wholesale trade can therefore be defined as the buying and selling of the goods in bulk (large quantities). A person who sells the goods in large quantities is known as a wholesaler. A wholesaler is a connecting link between the manufacturer and the retailer. Wholesalers play a crucial role in the supply chain, bridging the gap between manufacturers and retailers.

Functions of a Wholesaler:
- Buys goods in bulk from the manufacturer hence clearing the manufacturers' production line
- Warehouses the goods awaiting demand to ensure steady flow of goods, hence preventing price fluctuation (evens out prices) and allowing the manufacturer to produce the goods ahead of demand
- Breaks bulk and sells goods in smaller quantities to retailer
- Finances the retailer by providing credit to them and manufacturer by paying promptly
- Acts as an intermediary between manufacturer and retailer by passing information/complaints to manufacturers
- Provides a wide range of goods for the retailer and consumer which is gleened from different producers
- Prepares goods for sale by branding and blending them
- Provides transport (delivery) for goods from manufacturer and to the retailers premises
- Operates cash and carry warehouse
- Is a risk bearer, that is by storing the goods on behalf of the producer or retailer, the goods may go out of fashion or they may be gutted by fire or stolen whilst in the warehouse
- The types of wholesale trade are: cash and carry wholesaler, general wholesaler, specialist wholesaler and merchant wholesalers

Demonstrating Functions of a wholesaler:
- Sourcing products from manufacturers/suppliers at discounted prices
- Storage of products in the warehouse to ensure that the products are in good condition and accessible by the retailers
- Pricing goods at competitive prices and ensure that the wholesaler makes profit
- Finds buyers/retailers of the products and setting up distribution points
- Sells goods to retailers at competitive prices and delivers them to their premises

Opportunities:
- High profit margins as they buy goods in bulk at discounted rates from producers at factory prices
- They sell to retailers at a mark up
- Purchasing in bulk from the manufacturer reduces cost per unit which in return lowers cost and increased sales
- Supplying products to various retailers, distributors reduces dependance on a single customer or market
- Expanding product offering and customer base results in business growth and allows new market penetration
- Adjusting product mix and pricing strategies in response to the market changes
- Offers customised solution and services to the customers
- It builds mutual relationship with manufacturers
- It creates a reputation for quality products and reliable service, hence developing a strongbrand identity and customer loyalty
- Offers services such as labelling and packaging
- They warehouse goods bought in bulk from producers to keep prices stable
- They keep a steady supply of a variety of goods to retailers
- They bear risks of fashion change, fire, theft, whilst goods are in warehouses
- They offer credit facilities to retailers

Challenges:
- There is no personal contact with customers, and thus some customers may be lost to other wholesalers offering personal services
- Goodwill of the business may be adversely affected by lack of contact with customers
- Delivery services are not offered to retailers
- They face intense competition from other wholesaslers and online markets
- Sourcing high quality products from reliable suppliers
- Managing inventory levels, shipping and other logistics
- Dealing with supply chain disruptions, delays or shortages
- Setting competitive prices whilst maintaining a reasonable profit margin
- Managing price fluctuations, discounts and promotions
- Managing optimal inventory levels to meet demand
- Managing inventory turnover, storage and handling costs
- Dealing with stock unfashionableness, damage and theft
- Building and maintaining strong customer relationship
- Providing excellent customer service
- Handling customer complaints
- Staying uptodate with market trends, demand fluctuations and seasonal changes
- Adapting to changes in the consumer behaviour, preferences and spending habits
- Ensuring compliance with laws, regulations and standards
- Managing product safety, labeling and packaging requirements
- Implementing and intergrating technology solutions
- Managing data, cybersecurity and digital marketing

vocabulary.definition: An organization or individual that buys products in bulk and sells them to retailers or other businesses
An organization or individual that provides products or services to wholesalers, retailers or other businesses
An organization or individual that produces products for sale to wholesalers, retailers or other businesses
The stock of products held by a wholesaler or other business

questions.question: Mugubudu Wholesale Store buys boxes of pens and cases of sugar from a manufacturer and sells them to retailers in that area. What roles does the store play in the chain of distribution?
What are the benefits of the store buying pens directly from the manufacturer?
Describe how the retailers would be affected if Mugubudu Wholesale Store stopped its operations?
Investigate opportunities and challenges of running a wholesale business
Research on how to run a wholesale business successfully

sub_topics.expected_standard: Running a wholesale business practised successfully

sub_topics.title: Documents used in Home Trade
sub_topics.specific_competence: Prepare documents used in home trade

instructional_blocks.title: Describing and Preparing documents used in home trade
teacher_steps: Start by discussing learner's everyday experiences with trade documents they have seen at stores (receipt, cash sales slips)
Facilitate the discussion by asking prompt questions that will lead to the purpose and importance of different business documents used in home trade
Let the learners read a case study
Ask learners to identify the documents and their purpose
Learners to fill out and interpret the trade documents used in the case study and others that are important in home trade
Consolidate the lesson with content tips
learner_steps: Identify appropriate documents for different scenarios
Practice filling out various trade documents
Learn proper document formatting and details
instructional_blocks.case_study: Mr. Widson, the owner of Kasaropi ventures general limited calls Mr. Emmanuel, the owner of Gatoma investments limited based in Chipata asking about the motor bikes and spare parts in stock so that he orders for his shops in the northern region of the country. Mr. Emmanuel sends information about the goods in stock leading to Mr. Widson showing willingness to buy. Mr Emmanuel indicates that the goods can be sent using his truck or a hired truck. In a week's time a truck carrying the motor bikes and spare parts delivers at Mr. Widson's shops to his satisfaction though he was undercharged and some spare parts got damaged on the way.

instructional_blocks.title: Applying the terms and conditions of sale and payments (Trade discount and Cash discount)
teacher_steps: Assign a group of learners to demonstrate how buying and selling is done either at the market or at any trading area
Let others be buyers and others sellers
Let the learners do the role play while others observe
Ask learners to describe or say what they observed in the role play
Facilitate the discussion by asking prompt questions which will lead to what the lesson is all about (trade and cash discount)
Write their responses on the board
Work out an example on how to calculate trade and cash discounts
Consolidate the lesson as per content tips
Give learners exercises on how to calculate trade and cash discounts
learner_steps: Participate in role play as buyers or sellers
Identify trade and cash discount scenarios
Apply discount formulas to real scenarios

sub_topics.short_notes:
In any business transaction there are two parties involved, the buyer and the seller. Between the two parties there is a lot of communication, written and unwritten. Therefore, in home trade we need to have written documentation and these documents are evidences of what transpired in the business transaction. Understanding these documents is crucial to ensure smooth and successful transactions.

Documents used in home trade are important because they:
- Provide a paper trail, protecting the rights and interest of buyers and sellers
- Ensure that all parties have same access to the same information, reducing misunderstandings and disputes
- Help to ensure that the transaction complies with relevant laws and regulations

ENQUIRY NOTE:
- An enquiry is a request for information sent by the potential buyer/customer to the seller/supplier to find out on the availability of goods and services
- An enquiry may either be written or through verbal communication
- The potential buyer may want to know the description of goods, type and prices of goods, payment methods, delivery services, return policy and many more

THE QUOTATION:
- It is a reply to an enquiry to supply goods or services at a specified price
- It is sent by the supplier to the would-be buyer
- It contains detailed description of goods requested for, the price at which goods are offered, the terms and conditions of sale, payment and delivery dates

A quotation can come in the following ways:
1. Price list - A document that outlines the prices of a company's products or services. It gives the current prices of goods and services on sale
2. A Catalogue - Usually in form of a book, magazine, pamphlet or online content containing details and pictures of items currently being offered for sale
3. A Tender - A formal request for offers to supply goods, services or works at a specified price and within a defined timeframe
4. An Estimate - A detailed document provided by a contractor or service provider that outlines the expected costs for a specific project or job

AN ORDER:
- An order is a request to the supplier to supply goods quoted
- Orders may be written (Order Form or Ordinary Letter) or placed verbally on the phone
- An Order contains description of goods required, price as per quotation, quantity of goods, delivery date, terms and conditions of sale

PROFORMA INVOICE:
- A preliminary bill that a business sends to a customer before a sale is done
- It is a way to confirm details and costs of products or services before the transaction takes place
- Has the same details as those on a proper invoice
- The only difference is that a proforma invoice has words PROFORMA written on it

AN INVOICE:
- A document sent by the seller to the buyer requesting for payments for goods or services provided
- This document is simply a bill
- Can be used as proof that the transaction between the buyer and the seller has taken place
- Can be used in a court of law as a witness that there was a transaction
- It is a document that is also used to start the accounting process
- Shows detailed information such as description of goods, quantity supplied, unit and total prices of goods, terms of sales, Value Added Tax (VAT), E&OE (Errors and Omissions Expected)

An Advice Note:
- Sent to inform the buyer that the order has been received and the goods have been dispatched
- Allows the buyer to prepare for receiving the goods
- Usually sent ahead of the goods
- Specifies the method of transport used, and the date of delivery
- Usually shows the goods on the invoice

Delivery Note:
- Used when transporting goods to the buyer if the seller uses his or her own transport
- Sent by the seller to inform the buyer of delivery of goods
- Gives a detailed description of the goods including the quantity, type of goods and the condition in which goods have been received
- Helps the buyer to thoroughly check goods on their arrival

A Consignment Note:
- Document used when the seller is transporting goods using hired transport
- Shows the details of goods that have been sent from the seller to the buyer
- Used in business to record the details of goods being transported from one party to another
- Serves as proof of delivery of goods and provides trail for record keeping

Goods Received Note:
- A document that acknowledges or confirms the delivery of goods to a customer by the supplier
- Consists of a record of goods that the buyer has received
- Helps the customer to compare the goods delivered against the goods ordered

DEBIT NOTE:
- Written by the seller to the buyer if he has been undercharged
- Sent to the buyer to notify them of the current debt obligation
- Can be sent when: some items delivered were omitted on an invoice, buyer made an incorrect payment such as underpaying, price discrepancies, arithmetical errors

CREDIT NOTE:
- A document sent to the buyer to correct a mistake on an invoice or to refund an amount paid for
- Acts as a notification that a customer doesn't have to pay the total amount of an initial invoice
- Sent to a customer who has been overcharged
- Issued when the seller owes the buyer money
- Usually printed in red to distinguish it from a debit note
- Can be sent when: buyer returns goods or request for a refund, some items delivered are damaged or not ordered, buyer overpays an invoice

Cash sales slip:
- A document used in business to record the sale of goods or services for cash
- Records the sale, the amount paid and the method of payment
- Cash slips are obtained from computerised cash tills
- This document may not be preferred as a means of payment in business

Receipt:
- A document that serves as proof of payment for a transaction between a business and customer
- A record of the transaction showing the details of the purchase, the amount paid and the payment method used
- Also shows the date the payment is made

Cheque:
- A document that orders the bank to pay the amount specified to the recipient's account
- The person who writes and signs the cheque is called the drawer
- The person who receives the cheque/money is called a payee
- A cheque is not a legal tender, it can be accepted or rejected
- A cheque book has two parts: the cheque counter foil and a cheque

STATEMENT OF ACCOUNT:
- A financial document that summarises transactions between a customer and supplier over a period of time
- Provides customers with an overview of their purchase and what they are owing
- Purpose: remind customers of outstanding payment, provide transparency into transaction history, help customers reconcile their own financial records, facilitate communication

TRADE DISCOUNT:
- A reduction in the price of goods and services for bulk purchases
- A reduction in the list price of a product or service that a seller offers a buyer
- Usually given at the time of purchase
- Given to encourage customers to continue buying in bulk and enable them make profits
- Calculation: Trade Discount = Total Cost Price x Trade Discount Rate
- Net Price = Total Cost Price – Trade Discount

CASH DISCOUNT:
- A reduction in the amount due on an invoice or payment
- A reduction in price when there is prompt payment (quick payment)
- Offered by the seller to the buyer for paying the invoice promptly
- Given to encourage timely payments, reducing the amounts of outstanding accounts receivables
- Calculation: Cash Discount = Invoice amount x Discount rate
- Amount to be paid = Invoice amount – Cash discount

vocabulary.definition: Buying and selling of goods and services often with a goal of earning a profit
Buying and selling of goods and services within the country
A financial transaction between two or more parties that involve the exchange of goods, money, or services
A written record that provides evidence of a business transaction
The one in need of the goods and services in the process of buying and selling
The supplier of goods and services
A reduction in price of the product from the catalogue price given to the buyer
A reduction in price from the invoice price given to a customer

questions.question: Stephen wants to buy a laptop from Leah. Advise how he can obtain information?

Mr Kipyango a wholesaler sold a number of electrical appliances to a supermarket in Lusaka. Explain the importance of the following documents in the given transaction: a. Invoice b. Debit note c. Statement of account d. Advice note

Ms Lwito sold the following goods on credit to Mr Obby: 10 cases of milk at K150 per case, 15 bags of rice at K10,000, 2 cases of sugar at K520 per case. Mr Obby is allowed a 15% Trade discount and 5% cash discount if he paid within 20 days. i. How much would he pay if he settled his bill within 15 days? ii. How much will he pay if he decided to settle his bill on the 25th of the same month?
Use the information in question three (3) to prepare the following documents: i. An order ii. An Invoice iii. Delivery note

sub_topics.expected_standard: Documents used in home trade prepared successfully
